topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave of celebration washing over the ballroom. I was nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, trying to focus on the intricate floral arrangements, when I saw him. Across the room, leaning against a marble pillar, was Alex. My stomach clenched instantly, a cold knot tightening beneath my ribs. Every nerve ending seemed to short-circuit. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, and I instinctively dropped my glass, the crystal clinking loudly on the polished floor. I started to pivot, a clumsy, jerky movement, desperate to melt into the crowd, but my feet felt rooted to the spot. He looked up, and for a split second, the noise of the wedding vanished. His eyes met mine, and the air seemed to thin out. I mumbled something incoherent into my drink, my throat suddenly dry, and fled toward the exit, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing too loudly in the sudden silence of my mind.

[story 2]

I was midway through a tedious conversation with Sarah, trying to appear engaged, when I caught sight of him near the buffet table. It was impossible. Alex. The world tilted. A sudden, intense pressure built behind my eyes, making the bright lights of the reception feel abrasive. My hands began to tremble, the delicate silverware I was holding rattling against my palm. I forced a laugh, a sound that came out thin and brittle, while my gaze darted everywhere but toward him. Each step I took felt heavy, deliberate, as if wading through thick mud. When I finally managed to break free of the group, the urgency was a physical shove. I needed air, desperately needed distance, and I bolted toward the terrace doors, not caring where I went, just needing to escape the suffocating proximity of that one face.

[story 3]

The atmosphere was electric, a dizzying mix of laughter and forced merriment. I was trying to look casual, sipping my sparkling cider, when I turned a corner and collided, almost stumbling, right into him. The impact was minor, but it sent a jolt straight through me, a shockwave that made my vision swim slightly. For a beat too long, we just stood there, breathing the same air, the noise of the celebration receding into a dull roar. My mind went utterly blank. My mouth went dry, and I could feel a strange heat rising up my neck. I mumbled an apology, my voice sounding unnaturally high, and immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. I needed to disappear, to dissolve into the background noise, and I started walking away before I could formulate any coherent thought.
